Discuss investment characteristics, strategy implementation, and role in a portfolio of OPPORTUNISTIC hedge fund strategies

Distribute 90%–100% of otherwise taxable earnings to shareholders as dividends
Invest at least 75% of total assets in real estate
Derive at least 75% of gross income from real estate rental income or mortgage interest
US-specific: At least 100 shareholders; no five or fewer shareholders may own more than 50% of shares (the '5/50 rule')

Countries may also specify: maximum asset concentration, maximum non-rental income, maximum development activity, leverage limits Type Characteristics Self-managed (internal) Senior executives are company employees reporting to board elected by shareholders. Most US REITs. Fewer conflicts of interest — management incentives aligned with shareholders. Externally advised/managed Third-party adviser paid asset management fees, often based on AUM (incentive to grow size, not profitability). External managers may also charge for affiliated services (property management, acquisitions, debt placement). Higher conflict of interest. Region Notes North America REIT regime is most developed. Asia Pacific split roughly 49/51 — REOCs equally common. Europe dominate at 59% — REIT regime less developed in many countries. Advantages of REITs (vs. private) Disadvantages of REITs (vs. private)
Liquidity: Buy/sell almost any amount on major exchanges
Transparency: Readily available prices, transaction history, regulated disclosures
Property diversification: By type, geography, tenant credit quality
Professional management: Dedicated teams with economies of scale
Tax efficiency: Single taxation — REIT pays no corporate tax; only investor pays on dividends received
Low investment minimums; limited liability
Access to niche sectors: Data centres, medical offices, self-storage
Lack of retained earnings: Must access capital markets for growth — dilutive equity issuances if trading below NAV
High equity market correlation in short term: Public pricing reflects stock market sentiment, not just property fundamentals → reduces diversification benefit
Regulatory compliance costs: Burden of public company structure
Limited asset types: Restricted from large-scale development or non-qualifying services — must use Taxable REIT Subsidiaries (TRS) for these, which ARE subject to corporate tax
Higher volatility vs. private RE: Prices reflect stock market liquidity and sentiment
Dividends taxed at high ordinary income rates in some jurisdictions Historical cost accounting (US GAAP) distorts real estate valuations in two specific ways:
Understates carrying values: Long-held properties often appreciate in value (due to inflation or property- specific factors) but remain on the balance sheet at depreciated cost.

Overstates depreciation: Companies often use accelerated depreciation for tax purposes, further depressing accounting book value below economic value. IFRS offers companies a choice — the cost model (identical to PP&E) or the fair value model (all changes in fair value flow through net income). Under the fair value model, accounting values approach NAV. Under US GAAP historical cost, adjustment to NAV is always needed. NAVPS Definition: (Market Value of Assets − Market Value of Liabilities) / Shares Outstanding NAV = book value of assets REPLACED with market values, adjusted for: Included in NAV: Value of operating real estate (capitalised NOI) + cash + accounts receivable + land for development + prepaid/other tangible assets + non-asset-based income streams (fee/management income) + value added by management Excluded from NAV: Goodwill, deferred financing costs, deferred tax assets ('soft' assets), deferred tax liabilities ('soft' liabilities). These are accounting provisions, not economic items. The standard analyst process (illustrated in Exhibit 2 of the curriculum — Office REIT example): Step What to Do Step 1: Calculate Pro Forma Cash NOI Last 12-month NOI − Non-cash (straight-line) rent + Adjustment for full-year impact of mid-year acquisitions → Pro Forma Cash NOI Step 2: Estimate Next-12- Month Cash Pro Forma Cash NOI × (1 + growth rate). Growth estimated at ~1–3% based on analyst view. Step 3: Capitalise to get RE Value Estimated next-12-month cash NOI / Cap Rate = Estimated Value of Operating Real Estate. Cap rate derived from recent comparable property transactions. Step 4: Add other tangible assets + Cash & equivalents + Accounts receivable + Land held for future development (often at book if market value uncertain) + Prepaid/other tangible assets Step 5: Subtract liabilities − Total debt (at market value if materially different from face) − Other liabilities. Exclude deferred tax liabilities. Step 6: Divide by shares NAV / Shares Outstanding = NAVPS Worked Example — Exhibit 2 (Office REIT, USD thousands) Last-12-month NOI $270,432 Less: Non-cash (straight-line) rent ($7,667) — removes accounting fiction of averaged rent Plus: Full-year acquisition adjustment $4,534 — adds incremental 50% of annual return on mid- year acquisitions = Pro Forma Cash NOI (last 12 months) $267,299 Plus: Next-12-month growth at 1.5% $4,009 = Estimated Next-12-Month Cash NOI $271,308 ÷ Cap rate (7.00%) $271,308 / 0.07 = $3,875,829 + Cash and equivalents $65,554 + Land held for future development $34,566 + Accounts receivable $45,667 + Prepaid/other tangible assets $23,456 = Estimated Gross Asset Value $4,045,072 − Total debt ($1,010,988) − Other liabilities ($119,886) = Net Asset Value $2,914,198 ÷ Shares outstanding (55,689) Straight-line rent: Under IFRS/GAAP, companies recognise the AVERAGE contractual rent over the entire lease term, not actual cash received. In early years of a step-up lease, cash rent < accounting revenue; in later years, cash rent > accounting revenue. The straight-line adjustment removes this non-cash recognition so NOI reflects actual cash received — which is what matters for capitalisation. NAV is primarily used in Europe and Asia. US analysts more commonly use FFO/AFFO multiples. How to Interpret Premium/Discount to NAV Scenario Interpretation Trading AT NAV (P/NAV = 1×) Market values the REIT at its underlying property value. Neutral signal. Trading at PREMIUM to Higher NAV may be warranted if: (1) public market liquidity commands a lower required return; (2) management is creating above-average value (track record of superior acquisitions/developments); (3) access to capital at lower cost. Can range >25% premium at peak cycles. Scenario Interpretation Trading at DISCOUNT to May signal: undervaluation opportunity, governance concerns, high leverage, unfavourable asset mix, or anticipated NOI decline. Can range >25% discount in downturns. Practical use as relative tool If all REITs trade above NAV, compare P/NAV ratios — buy the smallest premium. If all trade at discount, sell the smallest discount. Also use implied cap rates: work backward from current stock price to solve for what cap rate the market is implying. Key limitations of NAV: (1) NAV is static/point-in-time — ignores management's future value creation potential. (2) Becomes subjective when markets are illiquid and few transactions are observable. (3) Large portfolios (hundreds of properties) are difficult to value individually. (4) Assets like undeveloped land, special-use properties, JVs, and service businesses complicate NAV accuracy. Net income for REITs is distorted by two major accounting adjustments that do not reflect economic reality:

Depreciation: GAAP/IFRS depreciate buildings over their useful life. But real estate often APPRECIATES in value. Depreciation deductions are economically misleading for income-producing property — they overstate the expense and understate true earnings.

Gains/losses on property sales: These are non-recurring. Including them in 'income' creates volatility that doesn't reflect sustainable operating performance. FFO formula:  FFO = Net Income + Depreciation & Amortisation − Net Gains on Sale of Real Property More complete Nareit definition: Net income (GAAP) + Losses (−Gains) from property sales + D&A on real estate + real estate impairments/write-downs unrelated to depreciation
Standardised by Nareit. SEC-accepted non-GAAP measure (like EBITDA) — must be reconciled to GAAP net income.
Widely used in US. Increasingly used in Asia Pacific. Europe/Asia still prefer NAV as primary.
FFO is NOT a cash flow measure — excludes investment and financing activities. Also includes FFO from unconsolidated businesses.

REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts): Directly own and operate properties. Must distribute nearly all earnings to avoid corporate income tax. Restricted to owning/operating rental properties or purchasing mortgages. Available in 30+ countries. Organized as corporations (US, Europe) or trusts (Australia).

Real Estate Management & Development: Firms engaged in operating, developing, or servicing real estate (REOCs — Real Estate Operating Companies). Taxable corporations with fewer restrictions, more flexibility. Cyclicality by property type: Healthcare REITs = more defensive (healthcare demand is stable). Industrial/office REITs = more cyclical (underlying tenants face volatile demand). Sensitivity depends on the TENANT's industry, not just the property type. Property Classes (Multi-family as Example) Class Description Class A Built within past 10 years or substantially renovated; top exterior/interior amenities; highest quality construction. Highest rents, LOWEST capital appreciation potential (already priced in). Class Description Class B Built within past 20 years or renovated; dated amenities; good construction with limited deferred maintenance. Class C Built within past 30 years; limited/dated amenities; aging construction with deferred maintenance. Class D Over 30 years, less desirable location; no amenities; poor condition. Lowest prices, HIGHEST capital appreciation potential if conditions improve. Sustainable Building Certification
BREEAM (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Methodology): UK-origin, prevalent in UK and Europe.
LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design): US-origin, most widely recognised global certification. China, Canada, India have most LEED projects outside US.
Both: Third-party, rating-based evaluation on energy/water efficiency, sustainable materials, transport access, carbon footprint.

Green premium: Higher build/renovation costs, but potentially lower operating costs, higher rents, lower vacancies, ESG appeal, and tax incentives in many jurisdictions. Form Vehicles Private Equity Direct ownership (sole, JV, LP) + Indirect (real estate funds, private REITs) Private Debt Mortgage debt, construction loans, mezzanine debt Public Equity Publicly traded shares (construction, operating, development companies), Public REITs, UCITs/Mutual Funds/ETFs Public Debt MBS/CMBS/CMOs, Covered bonds, Mortgage REITs, Mortgage ETFs Topic Detail REOCs vs REITs REOCs: Taxable corporations, fewer restrictions, can reinvest earnings. REITs: Tax-efficient pass-through structure, must distribute nearly all earnings, restricted to owning/operating rental properties. Covered bonds vs MBS Covered bonds: Issuer retains loan pool on balance sheet; dual recourse (both bank AND collateral); investor substitution rights. Lower risk/return. MBS: Securitized off balance sheet to SPV; tranched; prepayment risk; higher risk/return. Prepayment risk in MBS Contraction risk (early repayment) when rates fall. Extension risk (slower repayment) when rates rise. More prevalent in US due to lack of prepayment penalties common elsewhere.
Macroeconomic/market factors: Changes in economic activity, demographics, supply/demand balance, capital costs — affect market rents, occupancy, prices.
Management risk: Failure to maintain properties, manage tenant relationships, minimise vacancy.
Obsolescence risk: Renovation costs may exceed estimates; may not be economically viable to update older buildings.
Environmental/climate risk: Energy efficiency requirements, exposure to floods, earthquakes, hurricanes.
Technological/structural risk: Industry shifts (e-commerce vs retail, remote work vs office) create structural demand changes.

Algorithm/model risk (Zillow case study): Overreliance on statistical models ignoring qualitative local factors. Zillow Offers lost USD 1 billion before shutting down — could not scale home- buying/renovation/selling activities despite algorithmic valuations. Macroeconomic factors affect all real estate via demand for space and credit availability:

GDP growth: Increases demand for industrial, office, retail space (real estate as factor of production). Given long development lags, initial effect is higher rents and lower vacancies BEFORE supply catches
Job creation + wage growth: Drive household formation → demand for both rental housing and owner- occupied housing.

Credit conditions / interest rates: Critical because both residential and commercial are heavily debt- financed. Lower mortgage rates stimulate construction and purchase activity. Higher rates compress activity.

Housing prices vs income: When housing prices are low relative to household income + mortgage rates are low, homeownership becomes more attractive, reducing multi-family rental demand. The real estate cycle combines short-term rent/occupancy adjustments with long-term supply decisions. New supply lags demand signals — often completing AFTER market conditions have already changed. Phase Dynamics Recovery Business cycle trough. Little/no new construction (uncertain outlook, tight credit). Low occupancy. Owners cut lease rates to retain tenants. Downward pressure on rents and prices. Interest rates reach bottom; NOI reaches bottom; DSC reaches bottom; LTV peaks Phase Dynamics Expansion Economic growth + easing credit. Rising occupancy; owners can raise lease rates. New construction begins. Consumer confidence rises; demand for housing Interest rates rising; NOI rising; DSC increasing; LTV decreasing Oversupply Growth softens but construction pipeline completes. Property glut → occupancy declines, rents fall. Prices plateau then fall. Interest rates peak; NOI peaks then falls; DSC peaks then falls; LTV reaches bottom then rises Recession Economic slowdown + tightening credit. Accelerating occupancy decline. Landlords offer concessions. Construction starts at lows. Prices decline. Interest rates low; NOI falling; DSC decreasing; LTV increasing Exam trap — NOI vs DSC vs LTV timing: NOI PEAKS in oversupply (rents are still high as supply hits); DSC also peaks in oversupply. LTV reaches a LOW in oversupply (values near their peak). The relationship reverses in recession/recovery. Local economic factors determine how the macro cycle affects individual properties: business climate quality, presence of major employers, infrastructure, tax policy, zoning, transportation links, school quality, public safety. Properties in areas with declining industries or shrinking populations face structural headwinds regardless of the national cycle. Current Income Despite similarity to bond coupons, real estate lease income can be structured in several ways: Type Description Fixed rent Standard periodic payment — most similar to bond coupon. Type Description Step-up clause Pre-specified future rent increases (NOT contingent — unlike bond step-ups). Locks in rent increases on agreed schedule. Indexed rent Rent adjusts based on observed market variable (e.g., CPI). Links income to inflation. Overage / Percentage rent Base rent PLUS percentage of tenant sales above a breakeven level. Common in retail. Example: Chandra Shops — base EUR 135,000 + Rollover risk When holding period > lease term, owner faces risk of losing tenant and income gap during re-leasing. Capital Appreciation Property value increases contribute to total holding period return. Because real estate is illiquid and heterogeneous, price can only be precisely measured at the point of sale. Price estimation methods (income, cost, comparables, indexes) all involve approximation. Inflation Hedge Rents and property prices tend to rise with inflation — whether through indexed lease clauses explicitly or because physical assets preserve real value. Unlike fixed-income instruments with fixed nominal cash flows, real estate has natural pricing power in inflationary environments. Diversification Private real estate has historically shown LOW correlation with public equities and bonds, providing genuine portfolio diversification. However, PUBLIC real estate (REITs, MBS) exhibits HIGHER correlation with public markets in the short to medium term — the diversification benefit is primarily in private real estate. Tax Benefits
Depreciation: In some jurisdictions (US), private real estate can be depreciated faster than actual useful life, reducing taxable income.

REIT pass-through: REIT structures distribute income before tax to shareholders. Avoids double taxation (corporate tax + investor tax) common for regular corporations. Multi-family properties form the bulk of residential properties in commercial real estate categories. Categorised by location (urban/suburban), structure height (high-rise, garden apartments, townhouses), and amenities. Key residential-specific income metric: Equation 9 — GPRI:  Gross Potential Rental Income = Market Rent × Rentable Space GPRI is at FULL occupancy at current market rates. Actual income differs due to:
Loss to Lease: Difference between market rents and current contracted rents — negative when current leases are below market, positive when above market.
Vacancy & Collection Costs: Empty units + uncollected rent.

Concessions & Adjustments: Tenant incentives, free rent periods. Pinebranch Estates NOI (Example 6 — Multi-family) Add: Other income + Expense recovery Less: Rental deductions (vacancy, loss to lease, concessions) Less: Operating + Maintenance expenses Key contrast with Wallonia (single-tenant warehouse): Wallonia fully passes through property tax + insurance — cost increases don't reduce owner's NOI for those items. Pinebranch absorbs those costs (multi-tenant = can't pass through as easily). Multi-tenant properties also face much larger vacancy/concession deductions due to constant tenant turnover and short lease terms. Demand drivers: Local economic conditions, availability and affordability of owner-occupied alternatives. Tenants may have rent increase/eviction protections. Leases are much shorter than non- residential (month-to-month to 1 year vs. 5–10 years commercial). Office Properties
Range from major multi-tenant CBD buildings to single-tenant suburban facilities.
Often built for key tenant (anchor tenant reduces development risk).
Historically correlated with service industry employment (financial services, healthcare).

COVID-19 structural shift: Remote/hybrid work permanently reduced demand in many markets. JLL data: global leasing fell from 11m m² to 5m m² per quarter in Q2 2020, remained below pre-pandemic through Industrial and Warehouse Properties

Borrow or lend at the risk-free rate (rf) Binomial Model BSM / Black Model Binomial Model Discrete-time. Underlying moves to one of two values each period. Works backward from expiration. Can value American options (early exercise check). More flexible but computationally intensive for many periods. Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) Continuous-time. Closed-form formula. Assumes lognormal distribution. Only European options. Fast and elegant. Basis for the Black Model extensions. The binomial model assumes the underlying can move to only two values over each period:
u = up factor = S+ / S  (u > 1)
d = down factor = S− / S  (d < 1, typically d = 1/u)
S+ = uS  (up node),   S− = dS  (down node)
c+ = Max(0, S+ − X)  for a call;   p+ = Max(0, X − S+)  for a put

c− = Max(0, S− − X)  for a call;   p− = Max(0, X − S−)  for a put Step 1: Find the hedge ratio that creates a risk-free replicating portfolio (long Δ shares, short 1 call): Hedge Ratio:  Δ = h = (c+ − c−) / (S+ − S−) For puts: Δ = (p+ − p−) / (S+ − S−)  — this will be negative (short position in underlying) Why does Δ create a risk-free portfolio? The hedge ratio is chosen so that the portfolio value (Δ shares − 1 call) is IDENTICAL in both the up and down states. A portfolio with the same value in all future states earns exactly the risk-free rate — no arbitrage is possible.
Up state:  Δ(S+) − c+  =  Δ(S−) − c−  :Down state

Solving:  Δ(S+ − S−) = c+ − c−  →  Δ = (c+ − c−)/(S+ − S−) Step 2: Instead of the hedge ratio approach, we can use risk-neutral probabilities — the backbone of all no-arbitrage pricing: Risk-Neutral Probability (Up):  π = [(1 + r) − d] / [u − d] Down probability = (1 − π).  These are NOT real-world probabilities — they are the weights that make the expected return on the underlying equal to the risk-free rate. Key insight: π + (1 − π) = 1 (they sum to 100%). The option price is the present value of the expected payoff under risk-neutral probabilities. This eliminates the need to estimate real expected returns. One-Period Option Price:  c₀ = [π·c+ + (1−π)·c−] / (1+r) p₀ = [π·p+ + (1−π)·p−] / (1+r) Put-Call Parity:  S₀ + p = X/(1+r)ᵀ + c Use this as a sanity check: if you get c, solve for p using put-call parity and compare to the binomial result. Given: S = 100, X = 100, u = 1.35, d = 0.74, r = 5.15% Step 1 — Terminal payoffs:

S− = 100 × 0.74 = 74   →  c− = Max(0, 74−100) = 0   |  p− = Max(0, 100−74) = 26 Step 2 — Risk-neutral probability: π = (1.0515 − 0.74) / (1.35 − 0.74) = 0.3115 / 0.61 = 0.5107 Step 3 — Option prices: c₀ = [0.5107(35) + 0.4893(0)] / 1.0515 = 16.998  ≈ 17.00 p₀ = [0.5107(0) + 0.4893(26)] / 1.0515 = 12.10 Check via put-call parity: p = 16.998 + 100/1.0515 − 100 = 16.998 + 95.10 − 100 = 12.10  ✓ In a two-period recombining lattice, u then d gives the same price as d then u (i.e., S+− = S−+): Node Prices Call Payoffs (use Max(0, X−S) for puts) Nodes at T₂:
S++ = u²S (up-up)
S+− = udS (up-down = down-up)
S−− = d²S (down-down) Terminal Payoffs:
c++ = Max(0, u²S − X)
c+− = Max(0, udS − X)

c−− = Max(0, d²S − X) Always work from right to left (backward induction): Period 1 Call Prices (from Period 2 payoffs):  c+ = [π·c++ + (1−π)·c+−] / (1+r)   c− = [π·c+− + (1−π)·c−−] / (1+r) Then: c₀ = [π·c+ + (1−π)·c−] / (1+r) Shortcut — Direct Two-Period Formula:  c₀ = [π²c++ + 2π(1−π)c+− + (1−π)²c−−] / (1+r)² This is a binomial distribution weighting of the three terminal payoffs, discounted at (1+r)². Given: S = 50, X = 50, u = 1.356, d = 0.744, r = 5% Terminal nodes: S++ = 50(1.356)² = 91.94  →  c++ = 41.94 S+− = 50(1.356)(0.744) = 50.44  →  c+− = 0.4432 S−− = 50(0.744)² = 27.68  →  c−− = 0 Risk-neutral probability: π = (1.05 − 0.744) / (1.356 − 0.744) = 0.306/0.612 = 0.50 Direct formula: c₀ = [0.5²(41.94) + 2(0.5)(0.5)(0.4432) + 0.5²(0)] / 1.05² = [10.485 + 0.2216 + 0] / 1.1025 = 9.7112 Put via put-call parity: Delta changes at each node — you must rebalance the hedge each period: Period 1 Deltas:  Δ+ = (c++ − c+−) / (S++ − S+−)       [upper node at T₁] Δ− = (c+− − c−−) / (S+− − S−−)       [lower node at T₁] Δ at T₀: Δ₀ = (c+ − c−) / (S+ − S−) American Calls American Puts American CALLS No early exercise for non-dividend stocks: Early exercise forfeits the remaining time value. You ALWAYS do better selling the call than exercising it early (for non-dividend- paying underlyings). → American call = European call Exception: Deep-in-the-money calls on dividend- paying stocks — may be optimal to exercise just before the ex-dividend date to capture the dividend. American PUTS Early exercise CAN be optimal: For deep-in-the-money puts, the intrinsic value (X − S) may exceed the option's fair value. Exercising early captures this premium immediately, which can be reinvested at rf. American put > European put The difference is the early exercise premium. At each node, check:  Value = Max(European value, Intrinsic value) For American puts — at every intermediate node, compare: (1) Continuation value = [π·p+ + (1−π)·p−] / (1+r)   [hold the option] (2) Intrinsic value = Max(0, X − S)   [exercise immediately] American put value at node = Max(continuation, intrinsic) Given: S = 72, X = 75, u = 1.356, d = 0.541, r = 3%, 2-period At down-down node:  S−− = 72(0.541)² = 21.07  →  p−− = Max(0, 75−21.07) = 53.93 Intrinsic at down node:  X − S− = 75 − 38.95 = 36.05 European p− = [π(22.18) + (1−π)(53.93)] / 1.03 = 33.86 → Since Intrinsic (36.05) > European (33.86): EXERCISE EARLY Early Exercise Premium = 36.05 − 33.86 = 2.18 Put-Call Parity:  S₀ + p = X·e⁻ʳᵀ + c   (continuous)   or   S₀ + p = X/(1+r)ᵀ + c (discrete) If the left side ≠ right side, an arbitrage exists. Buy the underpriced portfolio, sell the overpriced. Overpriced Call Arbitrage Underpriced Call Arbitrage If c is too HIGH (LHS < RHS):
Sell (write) the call
Buy the put
Buy the stock
Borrow X·e⁻ʳᵀ If c is too LOW (LHS > RHS):
Buy the call
Replicating instruments are available and investable at known prices
No transaction costs, taxes, or market frictions
Short selling is permitted with full use of proceeds
Borrowing and lending at the same risk-free rate (r)

Risk-free rate is constant over the contract period Strategy Action Carry arbitrage (forward overpriced) Short (sell) the forward + Long (buy) the underlying using borrowed funds. Earn the difference risk-free. Reverse carry arbitrage (forward underpriced) Long (buy) the forward + Short the underlying + Lend the proceeds at rf. Earn the difference risk-free. No-arbitrage forward price The unique price at which neither strategy generates profit. Cost of carry = rf × S₀ × T. When the underlying pays no dividends, coupons, or storage costs — the forward price is simply the future value of the spot price: Forward Price (Periodic Compounding):  F₀(T) = S₀ × (1 + r)^T S₀ = spot price today; r = risk-free rate (periodic); T = time to delivery in years Forward Price (Continuous Compounding):  F₀(T) = S₀ × e^(rT) Use when rates are given as continuously compounded. Both forms are equivalent depending on convention. Real-world underlyings generate cash flows and involve storage costs. The general formula adjusts for these: General Carry Arbitrage Formula:  F₀(T) = [S₀ + PV(θ) − PV(γ)] × (1 + r)^T θ = carry costs (storage, insurance, convenience costs) — these ADD to F₀ γ = carry benefits (dividends, coupons, convenience yield) — these SUBTRACT from F₀ Intuition: The forward price = what it costs you to 'carry' the position to delivery. You must pay to borrow the money (cost = r × S₀), pay any storage/insurance costs (θ), but you receive any income from holding the asset (γ). After initiation, the forward's value changes as the spot price moves. To the LONG: Value of Long Forward (Periodic):  Vt(T) = St − F₀(T) / (1 + r)^(T−t) St = current spot price; F₀(T) = original forward price; (T−t) = remaining time to delivery Value of Long Forward (Continuous):  Vt(T) = St × e^−γ(T−t) − F₀(T) × e^−r(T−t) With continuous carry benefit γ (dividend yield). Short forward value = negative of long value. Time Value to Long At initiation (t=0) V₀ = 0. The forward price is set so both parties are indifferent. No cash changes hands. Time Value to Long During the contract (0 < t < T) V = St − PV(F₀). Long benefits when S rises above PV(F₀); short benefits when S falls. At expiration (t=T) VT = ST − F₀. The long profits if ST > F₀; the short profits if ST < Given: S₀ = 1,000, r = 2.5%, T = 0.75 years (9 months). No dividends. Forward price at initiation: Value 3 months later (t=0.25) when S = 1,050: Vt = 1,050 − 1,018.69 / (1.025)^0.50 = 1,050 − 1,006.24 = 43.76 Interpretation: The long has a mark-to-market gain of $43.76 per unit. The short has a loss of $43.76. Flows For a single stock paying known discrete dividends, remove the PV of dividends from S₀: Equity Forward Price — Discrete Dividends:  F₀ = [S₀ − PV(Dividends)] × (1 + r)^T PV(Div) = Σ [Dj / (1 + r)^tj] for each dividend Dj paid at time tj during [0, T] Worked Example: S₀ = CHF 70, T = 1 year, r = 1.0% Dividend CHF 2.20 paid at t = 0.5 years PV(Div) = 2.20 / (1.01)^0.5 = 2.20 / 1.00499 = 2.1891 For equity indices paying a continuous dividend yield δ: Equity Index Forward (Continuous Yield):  F₀ = S₀ × e^(r−δ)T δ = continuous dividend yield of the index. Higher δ → lower F₀ (dividends reduce the cost of carry). Value of Equity Index Forward:  Vt = St × e^−δ(T−t) − F₀ × e^−r(T−t) St = current index level; discounts both spot (at δ) and F₀ (at r) to present value. Example — EURO STOXX 50: S₀ = 3,000, δ = 3.00%, r = 0.15%, T = 0.25 (3 months) F₀ = 3,000 × e^(0.0015 − 0.03) × 0.25 = 3,000 × e^(−0.007125) = 3,000 × 0.99290 = 2,978.71 Observation: F₀ < S₀ because the dividend yield (3%) exceeds the risk-free rate (0.15%). The index forward trades at a discount to spot. For bond forwards, the underlying generates coupon cash flows (γ = PV of coupons). Accrued interest must be handled carefully: Fixed-Income Forward — Full Price:  F₀(Full) = [S₀(Full) + AI₀ − PV(Coupons)] × (1 + r)^T − AIT AI₀ = accrued interest at initiation; AIT = accrued interest at delivery; PV(Coupons) = PV of coupons paid during [0,T] Quoted (Clean) Forward Price:  QF₀ = F₀(Full) − AIT Market convention quotes the CLEAN (quoted) price. AIT is subtracted to remove embedded accrued interest at delivery. Bond futures are standardized and quote a 'quoted futures price'. The invoice price paid at delivery involves a conversion factor (CF): Concept Formula / Explanation Quoted futures price (QF₀) QF₀ = F₀ / CF   where F₀ is the full forward price Invoice price Invoice = QF₀ × CF + AIT   (what the short receives at delivery) Conversion factor (CF) Price of the deliverable bond per $1 face value if yield = 6%. Standardizes bonds to common pricing. Cheapest-to-Deliver (CTD) The bond the short chooses to deliver. Short picks whichever bond minimizes: (Quoted price − QF₀ × CF). Always verify by comparing CTD candidates. CTD Intuition: The short in a bond futures contract wants to deliver the cheapest bond. Bond A is CTD if: Quoted_A − QF₀ × CF_A < Quoted_B − QF₀ × CF_B. The short delivers CTD, so pricing should use the CTD bond's characteristics.

15. Common Pitfalls & Exam Tips Context: Using AstraZeneca as an example, wages and salaries represent 30% of total expenses—a material portion that requires careful analysis. Five Main Categories Category Timeline Key Examples Short-Term Benefits < 12 months Wages, salaries, bonuses, paid leave Long-Term Benefits > 12 months Long-term disability, deferred comp Termination Benefits On termination Severance packages, golden parachutes Post-Employment After retirement Pensions, retiree healthcare Share-Based Ongoing/vesting Stock options, RSUs, restricted stock I Key Point: Share-based compensation and post-employment benefits are the most complex from both accounting and valuation perspectives. A. Restricted Stock Common shares granted to employees with selling restrictions. Typically used for highly compensated employees to align interests and aid retention. Type Voting Dividends Tradeable Restricted Stock Performance Shares Restricted Stock Units Key Distinctions:
Performance Shares: Restricted stock where vesting depends on achieving specific performance targets (e.g., EPS > $5.00)

RSUs: Represent the right to receive shares, not actual shares. No dividends or voting rights until vesting. B. Stock Options Non-tradeable call options on the employer's stock, typically granted at-the-money. Characteristics:
Strike price usually equals current share price at grant
Employee gains (Share Price - Strike) upon exercise

Must exercise after vesting but before expiry Grant date: Stock trading at $50, options granted with $50 strike Vesting: 3 years Exercise: 5 years after grant, stock now at $75 Employee profit: ($75 - $50) × number of options = $25 per option C. Other Share-Based Instruments

15. Key Exam Tips Three Key Currency Concepts: Presentation Currency: The currency in which financial statements are presented. Typically the currency of the country where the company is located. Functional Currency: The currency of the primary economic environment in which an entity operates. The currency in which the entity primarily generates and expends cash. Local Currency: The currency where the company operates. For subsidiaries, this is the currency of the host country. I Key Point: In most cases, all three are the same currency. However, for multinationals with foreign subsidiaries, these currencies differ, requiring translation.
